title: Create, copy, move and rename

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Create, copy, move and rename; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Pro Git; second edition online; section applicable to Create, copy, move and rename
sources.claim: Creating, copying, moving, renaming, and deleting files changes paths and content independently; each operation should name an exact source and destination and be verified afterward.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Creating, copying, moving, renaming, and deleting files changes paths and content independently; each operation should name an exact source and destination and be verified afterward.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A source file is moved with Git-aware tooling, then status confirms one rename rather than an accidental deletion plus unrelated new file. Your goal is to reorganize a disposable project tree and verify every resulting path. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf tools files; List, read and search. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Creating, copying, moving, renaming, and deleting files changes paths and content independently; each operation should name an exact source and destination and be verified afterward. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Overwrites, recursive operations, links, permissions, case-only renames, and cross-filesystem moves have platform-specific behavior and can cause irreversible loss. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: Move and copy are interchangeable because both make the file appear at the destination.

includes: Manipulate files and directories with quoted literal paths and verify destinations.
Reorganize a disposable project tree and verify every resulting path.
excludes: Overwrites, recursive operations, links, permissions, case-only renames, and cross-filesystem moves have platform-specific behavior and can cause irreversible loss.

outcomes: Reorganize a disposable project tree and verify every resulting path.

examples.title: Create, copy, move and rename: direct case
examples.situation: A source file is moved with Git-aware tooling, then status confirms one rename rather than an accidental deletion plus unrelated new file.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A source file is moved with Git-aware tooling, then status confirms one rename rather than an accidental deletion plus unrelated new file.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Copy preserves the source while move changes its location; verify both endpoints and repository status before treating the operation as complete.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Copy preserves the source while move changes its location; verify both endpoints and repository status before treating the operation as complete.

examples.title: Create, copy, move and rename: boundary case
examples.situation: A new directory is created before copying a template, and the destination is inspected before any original is removed.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: A new directory is created before copying a template, and the destination is inspected before any original is removed.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Overwrites, recursive operations, links, permissions, case-only renames, and cross-filesystem moves have platform-specific behavior and can cause irreversible loss.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: Move and copy are interchangeable because both make the file appear at the destination.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Reorganize a disposable project tree and verify every resulting path.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Move and copy are interchangeable because both make the file appear at the destination.
misconceptions.correction: Copy preserves the source while move changes its location; verify both endpoints and repository status before treating the operation as complete.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: Move and copy are interchangeable because both make the file appear at the destination.

analogy.example: A labeled filing room can model Create, copy, move and rename: current location changes how relative directions resolve, and moving differs from copying a folder.
usefulFor: Reasoning about exact locations, inspection, file operations, and destructive scope
limitations: Shell expansion, permissions, links, filesystem rules, and command execution can behave unlike physical folders.
Overwrites, recursive operations, links, permissions, case-only renames, and cross-filesystem moves have platform-specific behavior and can cause irreversible loss.

artifact: A source file is moved with Git-aware tooling, then status confirms one rename rather than an accidental deletion plus unrelated new file.

Copy preserves the source while move changes its location; verify both endpoints and repository status before treating the operation as complete.

scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.

recap.summary: You should now be able to reorganize a disposable project tree and verify every resulting path. Keep this boundary in view: Overwrites, recursive operations, links, permissions, case-only renames, and cross-filesystem moves have platform-specific behavior and can cause irreversible loss.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Create, copy, move and rename without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: Move and copy are interchangeable because both make the file appear at the destination.
Apply the model to this case from memory: A new directory is created before copying a template, and the destination is inspected before any original is removed.
